text: Welcome to another installment of robotic hernia surgery. Today, we're talking about the repair of a large spagalian hernia. This was a really interesting case. A sixty-year-old man was presented with a chronic bulge for many years. He had increased abdominal pain around the bulge and his general practitioner got a CT scan. Here's the cross-sectional imaging. Pretty classic findings of a spagalian hernia. It's dissecting between the external and internal obliques, and you'll see bilateral fat containing direct hernias that were asymptomatic.

The laparoscopic camera reveals a large Spigelian hernia defect measuring approximately six by five centimeters, dissecting the left abdominal wall and containing colon, small bowel, and omentum. The surgeon manually reduces most of the incarcerated contents, leaving the descending colon drawn up with the retroperitoneum, anticipating it will descend with the hernia sac. A robust preperitoneal fat pad is visible.

The surgeon begins preperitoneal dissection, working circumferentially around the hernia defect and the neck of the hernia sac. The dissection proceeds to the lateral abdominal wall, taking down areolar tissue and approaching the medial margin of the hernia. The rectus abdominis muscle is visible. The surgeon reduces the hernia sac, focusing on the inferior and medial aspects, using two working arms. The hernia sac reduces smoothly due to minimal chronic adhesions.

The surgeon invaginates the hernia sac to prevent it from falling back into the space, then performs cephalad dissection before completing the reduction of the hernia sac. The circumferential dissection includes the myopectineal orifice on the patient's left side, involving the spermatic vessels. The direct hernia is visible and reduced, providing good coverage of the Spigelian defect. The right groin is not accessed, and the right asymptomatic hernia is not addressed. The Spigelian defect and reduced direct hernia are clearly visible, with sufficient dissection around the myopectineal orifice.

The surgeon prepares for defect closure and mesh placement, using an 18-inch permanent V-lock suture on a GS-XXII for closure. A port is introduced cephalad for anticipated mesh placement. Sequential tightening and traction facilitate defect closure with reduced insufflation pressure. Mid-weight meshes are placed, with an extra-large piece covering the groin and additional mesh covering the retromuscular and preperitoneal space, ensuring at least 10 centimeters of coverage in all directions.

The surgeon places multiple interrupted stitches to secure the mesh in position, using fibrin sealant for additional fixation. The flap closure around the hernia sac is performed, despite challenges due to proximity to the surgical site. The mesh is successfully excluded from the viscera, and a trocar is used to release preperitoneal air and pass a drain into the hernia space. The procedure concludes with the patient being prepared for overnight observation and discharge with the drain.
